https://github.com/Zettlr/Zettlr/commit/.commit_message: [Performance] Refactor citation parser to use sets (#5986)

description.text: ## Description This PR fully rewrites the citation parser to now actually add appropriate SyntaxNodes into the parse tree that can in the future be used by various parts in the code to extract and render citations. "Why the f\*\*\* would you rewrite a perfectly working system?!" Well, because I had my gripes with the way citations were handled up until now. To begin with, the existing citation parser still stems from CodeMirror 5 days, when adding new syntax nodes was not straight forward. It cuts many edges, is slow, and mounts single, monolithic citation nodes into the tree with no proper subdivision of the various citation elements. The new Markdown parser allows for a better inline parser for adding additional syntax elements, and so having a more accurate and up-to-date parser has a ton of benefits, including, but not limited to: * Now all elements of citations are mounted as proper SyntaxNodes. This includes the citation formatting characters (brackets, the @-sign), prefix and suffix, citekey, and locator. * This means that CSL Citation Items (which are required by citeproc.js to prerender citations and construct bibliographies) can now be fully constructed by only looking at the parse trees -- no more additional regular expression wizardry required. ~~(This is still todo)~~ * In addition, I now enabled the parser to finally pick up on weird citekeys and complex locators that Pandoc allows using bracketed syntax (e.g., `@{a complex citekey with otherwise unallowed characters}`) * Every piece of a citation can now be individually styled, if one so wishes (see the screenshots below for some examples that I used during testing) * This further cements the parse tree as the single source of truth for information about a document. No more additional parsing required, because citations are no longer mounted as individual, monolithic syntax nodes. ~~However, this still needs to be implemented in some parts of the application (hence, only a draft PR).~~ * This, incidentally, also allows users to turn off citation parsing and still detect whether a citation looks correct. * Furthermore, the previous method of citation parsing was computationally very intensive, because many parts of the document had to be parsed over and over to find citations with complex regular expressions and in general some pretty complex computations. Now, it's as fast as it can practically get. ## Changes The changes are huge, but from a theoretical standpoint not that big. Citations are now parsed using a relatively basic character-based parser that simply moves through the inline context and tries to parse a citation until it finds a stop condition. I additionally took the liberty of aggressively checking for potential stop-conditions to abort parsing as early as possible once a citation can be guaranteed to be invalid. This makes the parser much more efficient, and also aligns it more with the intentions of Marijn's parser that is intended to be run character for character. ## Additional information Some screenshots demonstrating the individual syntax nodes and showing a few edge cases: I have created those screenshots with the following temporary rules in the Berlin theme: ```ts '.cm-citation': { textDecoration: 'underline' }, '.cm-citation-mark': { fontFamily: 'monospace', color: '#6633ff' }, '.cm-citation-prefix': { color: 'var(--grey-1)' }, '.cm-citation-suppress-author-flag': { color: 'red' }, '.cm-citation-at-sign': { fontFamily: 'monospace' }, '.cm-citation-citekey': { color: 'var(--green-0)' }, '.cm-citation-locator': { fontFamily: 'monospace', color: 'red' }, '.cm-citation-suffix': { color: 'var(--grey-1)' }, ``` ## TODOs There are a few remaining todos: - [x] Utilize the parse tree information to render citations in the citation renderer - [x] Utilize the parse tree information to generate citations in the MarkdownAST - [x] Fully sherlock and remove the old citation parser, as it is no longer needed *** Tested on: macOS Tahoe 26
